topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to vibrate under my gaze. A C-minus. For the history paper, the one I’d poured three sleepless nights into, I had somehow managed this dismal result. I picked up the graded essay, the crisp paper feeling impossibly heavy in my trembling hands. My stomach clenched into a tight knot, and I could feel a flush creeping up my neck, turning my cheeks a painful shade of crimson. I reread the feedback, the professor’s terse comments highlighting my lack of critical analysis. Every word felt like an accusation. I shoved the paper into my backpack with unnecessary force, the sound of the zipper echoing too loudly in the quiet library aisle. I just wanted to disappear.

[story 2]

"A D-? Seriously?" I whispered to myself, the sound barely audible over the hum of the fluorescent lights. My jaw tightened, and I stared blankly at the numerical grade displayed on the screen. It wasn't just a grade; it was a public declaration of my inadequacy. I ran a hand through my already messy hair, pulling at the strands as if to physically pull the failure out of my system. My palms grew slick. I could feel the heat radiating from my face, a deep, uncomfortable warmth that made me want to sink into the nearest chair and simply cease existing. I quickly minimized the grading portal, desperate to escape the stark reality of the poor performance.

[story 3]

When Professor Albright announced the results, the air in the lecture hall seemed to thin. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out everything else. When my name was called and the low, disappointing mark was read aloud, a sudden coldness washed over me, followed instantly by a burning sensation in my chest. I looked down at my notes, suddenly seeing only scribbles and errors, a testament to my failure. I mumbled a barely recognizable "thank you," my voice sounding thin and reedy, and I made a swift, clumsy move to gather my things, desperate to vacate the space before anyone could look too closely at the evidence of my poor effort.
